segments.s420-g41: A compact noun phrase: همان ('the same') modifies عهد ('pledge, oath'), which is further specified by the name کاوس (Kay Kavus). دارم is the 1st person singular present 'I have/hold'; نخست ('first, at the outset') is an adverbial qualifier placed after the verb for emphasis — together: 'I hold the same pledge to Kay Kavus from the start.'
segments.s420-g42: که introduces a subordinate (that/which) clause. بر من is the prepositional phrase 'against me'. بهانه 'excuse, pretext' is the object. نیارند is a poetic/older negative form corresponding to نیاورند (3rd pl. subjunctive/optative: 'may they not bring' / 'they should not bring'). جست here is a light verb meaning 'to seek/try' used with بهانه — 'to seek/bring an excuse' — the whole clause: 'that they bring no excuse against me.'
segments.s420-g43: Another noun phrase: همان ('the same') modifies عهد ('covenant/oath'), and کیخسرو دادگر is an apposition naming the ruler (Kay Khosrow) with the epithet دادگر 'the Just' (used here as a title/epithet). The whole phrase picks out a specific pledge associated with that king.
segments.s420-g44: که again begins a relative/explanatory clause. چون او 'like him' (چون = 'like/as'), نبست is here negative past (ن + بست) meaning 'did not bind' or in idiomatic sense 'no one bound/ girt (their) loins like him'; از کیان 'from/among the Kians' marks the group, کس 'anyone' supplies the indefinite/negative scope, and کمر 'girdle/waist' is the object. Word order is poetic: verb (نبست) appears before the indefinite کس.
segments.s420-g45: زمین را is the definite object/topic 'the land' marked by را; سراسر همه is an adverbial intensifier 'throughout entirely.' گشته‌ام is the 1st person perfect/resultative 'I have gone/roamed' (گشتن used here in the sense 'to go about/roam'), so the phrase means 'I have gone throughout the land.'
segments.s420-g46: بسی is a quantifier 'much/many' (classical usage), followed by شاه بیدادگر 'tyrant kings' (بیدادگر modifying شاه). کشته‌ام is the 1st person perfect of کشتن 'I have killed / I have slain' (transitive past perfect), so the clause states 'I have slain many tyrant kings.'
segments.s420-g47: چو 'when' introduces a temporal clause. من is the subject 'I'. برگذشتم is past 1st singular 'I crossed/ passed over'. ز جیحون 'from the Jeyhun' gives the river crossed, and بر آب 'over the water' is a locative phrase emphasizing the crossing; overall: 'When I crossed the Jeyhun over the waters...'.
segments.s420-g48: ز توران 'from Turan' marks origin; به چین 'into (the land of) Chin/China' marks destination. آمد is the simple past 'came', and the proper name افراسیاب follows the verb (poetic word order inversion): 'Afrasiab came from Turan to Chin.'
segments.s420-g49: ز here is the poetic preposition meaning "from" and begins a prepositional phrase describing separation or departure ("from Kay Kavus"). در جنگ هاماوران is a locative/temporal adjunct ("in/at the battle of Hamavaran"); word order is noun phrase + adjunct, typical in classical Persian narrative.
segments.s420-g50: به تنها برفتم combines a directional preposition + adverb + verb: به + place (here introducing direction) + تنها "alone" as an adverbial, and برفتم is the past first‑person verb meaning "I went" (an archaic/epic form with prosthetic b‑). به مازندران names the destination. Together: "I went alone to Mazandaran."
segments.s420-g51: نه marks negation in a repeated, enumerative way: "neither... nor..." followed by the noun and the verb ماندم "remained/was left". The sequence "نه ارژنگ ماندم نه دیو سپید" literally says "neither Arzhang remained, nor the White Demon." Word order places the negator before each conjunct, and ماندم is the past intransitive 'remained'.
segments.s420-g52: Continuation of the same negative list with repeated نه: the speaker enumerates further things that did not remain. The verbs are omitted because they are carried over from the previous clause; this is a standard enumerative style in classical Persian.
segments.s420-g53: همی is a habitual/adverbial particle meaning "continually/always." از پی together means "after/in pursuit of" (از + پی); شاه فرزند را is the definite object (را marks the object), literally "the king's son". The phrase sets up what is being pursued.
segments.s420-g54: بکشتم here is the past tense verb meaning "I killed/slain" (an archaic epic past form; the prosthetic b‑ is common in classical Persian). The following nouns دلیر خردمند را are the object phrase modified by two adjectives/epithets ("the brave, the wise") and are marked by the object clitic را. Thus the clause means "I slew the brave, the wise one(s)."
segments.s420-g55: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause ("for/that"). گردی here functions as 'a man / a hero' in older epic usage (the supplied lexicon entries don't include this sense), چو means "like," and سهراب is the proper name. هرگز نبود is the negative past "never was" — together: "for there never was a man like Sohrab."
segments.s420-g56: This clause lists the domains in which Sohrab was unrivaled, each introduced by به to mark the sphere/instrument: به زور "in/with strength," به مردی "in manliness/valor," و رزم "and in battle." آزمود is the past participle/verb "tested/tried/experienced." The coordination with و links the three spheres.
segments.s420-g57: ز here is the archaic/pre-classical preposition meaning 'from' or 'over' (used to mark a quantity exceeded). همانا is an emphatic adverb 'verily/indeed'. فزونست is a contracted poetic form (فزون + است) meaning 'is more' / 'exceeds' with the archaic enclitic -ست. سال is the noun 'year' placed after the predicate to complete the temporal expression.
segments.s420-g58: که تا introduces the temporal subordinate clause ('since / from the time that'); تا is the temporal 'until/since' here. من is the 1st-person pronoun 'I'. جدا is an adjective/adverb 'apart, separated' that combines with گشتم (past of گشتن) to mean 'I became separated / I parted'. از marks the origin 'from'. پشت زال literally 'the back of Zāl' (پشت = back/behind), here indicating separation from Zāl (physically or metaphorically).
segments.s420-g59: همی is an older adverb meaning 'still/always' or continuous aspectual marker. پهلوان بودم is a simple past copular clause 'I was a hero/champion' (بودم = past of بودن). اندر جهان is the locative phrase 'in the world' (اندر is an older preposition meaning 'in/within') placed after the verb phrase in the poetical word order.
segments.s420-g60: یکی بود = 'there was one / someone existed' (یکی here indefinite 'a certain person' / 'someone'). با links that person to the following description. آشکارم is آشکار + ـم (the adjective/noun 'manifest/visible' with 1st-person possessive suffix '-m'), i.e. 'my manifest/outward [side]'; نهان functions as 'hidden' (understood as 'hidden side' or 'secret'). The phrase means 'there was someone who knew/was privy to both my open and my secret [aspects]'.
segments.s420-g61: به سان is a formula meaning 'like / in the manner of' (به + سان). The proper name فریدون and epithet فرخ‌نژاد ('well-born' / 'fortunate-born') form the simile's referent: 'like Fereydun Farrokh-nâzhâd.'
segments.s420-g62: که is the relative 'who'. تاج بزرگی is 'a crown of largeness' (بزرگی here expresses size/largeness, i.e. 'a large/great crown'). به سر is the locative phrase 'onto the head' (به = to/on; سر = head). بر نهاد is the past verb 'he placed' (نهاد = placed/put, with the preverb بر 'upon'), so the verb comes at the end in the poetic word order: 'who placed a great crown upon his head.'
segments.s420-g63: ز تخت 'from the throne' uses the archaic preposition ز = 'from' with تخت 'throne'. اندرآورد is a compound verb (اندر + آورد) meaning 'to bring in/out' or 'to remove/bring down' here (i.e. remove from the throne). ضحاک را: ضحاک is the proper name Zahhāk and را is the definite object marker marking him as the patient of اندرآورد.
segments.s420-g64: سپرد is the past tense 'he entrusted/handed over'. آن سر و تاج او is a coordinated definite object phrase 'that head and his crown' (آن = that; او = his). خاک را places the destination/recipient 'to the earth' with را marking خاک as a definite target: 'he consigned that head and his crown to the earth.' Note the poetic object/recipient ordering.
segments.s420-g1: بدو is the prepositional clitic meaning “to him/unto him” placed before the verb; گفت is the reporting verb (past of گفتن) introducing direct speech; که here introduces the content of that speech; آرام گیر is an idiomatic two-word command, where آرام (calm) + گیر (an archaic imperative form) together mean “be calm.”
segments.s420-g2: چه is the interrogative “what/why” starting the question; گویی is the 2nd‑person present/optative form (poetic) meaning “you say/you are saying”; سخنهای is the plural noun “words/speeches” (سخن + plural -ها); نادلپذیر is an adjectival compound (نـ + دلپذیر) meaning “unpleasant/unacceptable,” modifying سخنهای.
segments.s420-g3: دلت is دل + enclitic -ت “your heart” (possessive suffix); بیش before the noun gives the sense “more / too much”; کژی is a substantive “crookedness / wrongness”; بپالد is an archaic verbal form (ب‑ prefix + پالَدن) meaning “nurtures / cherishes” (here in a habitual/optative sense); همی is a habitual adverb “ever/continually.”
segments.s420-g4: روانت is روان + -ت “your soul/spirit”; ز is the poetic preposition “from / because of”; دیوان here refers to the court/diwan (the royal office or court) and functions as the cause of pride; ببالد is a present/habitual form “becomes proud / takes pride”; همی again marks habitual/continuous action.
segments.s420-g5: تو is the 2nd‑person pronoun “you”; آن گوی is a predicative phrase “that (one) / such a person” (گوی here functions as a nominal element meaning a thing/person so described); کز is the relative particle “that/which”; پادشاهان is the plural “kings”; سزاست is a compound of سزا (befit/deserve) + است, meaning “is fitting / is deserved.”
segments.s420-g6: نگوید here functions as a present third‑person negative/optative form meaning “does not say / one does not say”; سخن پادشا is a genitive phrase “the speech/words of a king”; جز introduces the exception “except”; که راست is a relative clause “that which is true,” so the whole means the king utters nothing except what is true.
segments.s420-g7: جهاندار is the title 'the sovereign / ruler' and is the subject; داند is the present form of دانستن “knows” (archaic spelling); که introduces the content clause; دستان سام is a possessive construction where دستان (here meaning tales/deeds/feats) refers to Sam’s exploits; سام is the proper name Sam.
segments.s420-g8: بزرگست is a fused predicate meaning “is great”; و is the coordinating conjunction “and”; بادانش is a fused form = با دانش (“with knowledge”) used adjectivally to mean “learned/knowledgeable”; the final و نیک‌نام adds another coordinated predicate “and well‑reputed / of good name.”
segments.s420-g73: This whole clause expresses a report + purpose: گفتم (I said) is the main verb and the phrase تا بدانی introduces a purpose clause 'so that you may know'. The initial بدان is an archaic topical/locative/cliticized element ('to that / to him' or 'that') used in older Persian; بدانی is the present-subjunctive 'you may know' (تا + subjunctive = purpose), and همه is the object 'everything'. Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic emphasis (topic before verb).
segments.s420-g74: تو شاهی is a nominal predicate: تو (you) + شاهی (kingship / you are king) with the copula omitted (normal in Persian). و links the second clause; گردنکشان is a plural noun 'the neck‑raisers' (i.e. rebels/proud ones) and چون رمه is a simile 'like a flock'.
segments.s420-g75: Subject (تو) + prepositional phrase اندر زمانه (in the age) + رسیده (arrived/has come) used predicatively, followed by نوی (new) qualifying زمانه; the past participle/perfect رسیده functions as a stative/result ('you have come into') — classical word order places the locative phrase before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s420-g76: اگر چند is a concessive phrase 'if/though (even) a little' (classical usage = 'even if' or 'though'), با + فر indicates accompaniment/possession 'with the far (royal glory)', and کیخسروی qualifies فر as 'of Keykhosrow'. The phrase means 'even if briefly/only, with the royal glory of Keykhosrow'.
segments.s420-g77: تن خویش (your own body) uses خوی + the enclitic ش to form the reflexive/possessive 'one's own'; the object precedes the verb بینی (you see), which is second‑person singular present. همی is an adverb 'always/still', and در جهان is the locative phrase 'in the world' — overall SOV order with topicalized object typical in poetic Persian.
segments.s420-g78: نه‌ای + predicate: here نه‌ای is an archaic negative/coptula construction meaning 'you are not' (classical 'نه‌ای' = 'thou art not / there is not'), followed by آگه 'aware' and از introducing the domain 'of' (از کارهای نهان = 'of hidden affairs'). The clause therefore reads 'you are not aware of hidden deeds'.
segments.s420-g79: چو here is a temporal conjunction 'when'; بسیار شد is a past analytic 'became many' (شد = became), with گفتها ('sayings, words') as the subject that has multiplied. می‌خوریم is 1st person plural present/habitual 'we drink' (mi‑ prefix + ‑یم ending); in narrative poetry this can render a habitual or past action.
segments.s420-g80: به marks purpose/benefit 'for' or 'to'; می‌جان is a compound/poetic phrase (here functioning like 'wine and life' or a term of endearment tied to wine); اندیشه را is the definite/accusative object (را marks the direct object 'the thought'); بشکریم is an optative/jussive 1st‑person‑plural form with the prefixed ب‑ used in classical Persian for subjunctive/volitional sense — here meaning roughly 'we would break/scatter (or set aside) thought' (the exact verbal root is poetic/ambiguous).
segments.s420-g33: همان is a demonstrative 'that' pointing back to a previously mentioned person; مادرم is مادر + enclitic -م 'my' (possessive). دخت is an archaic/poetic form meaning 'daughter'. مهراب here is a proper name (the father/household name). بود is the past copula 'was' that closes the clause. Word order is topic (همان مادرم) + predicate (دخت مهراب بود).
segments.s420-g34: بدو is the dative/prepositional form 'to her / for her' (indicating possession or benefit). کشور هند = 'the country/realm of India' (کشور = country, هند = India). شاداب 'prosperous/vigorous' modifies کشور هند. بود is the past copula 'was', so the clause literally says 'to her the country India was vigorous.'
segments.s420-g35: که is the relative conjunction 'that/who' linking this clause to the previous. ضحاک is the proper name Zahhak. بودیش is an archaic copular/possessive form meaning 'was his' / 'belonged to him' (i.e., 'was his [forefather]'). پنجم پدر = 'fifth father' i.e. 'fifth forefather/ancestor'. The clause says 'for whom Zahhak was his fifth forefather.'
segments.s420-g36: ز here is the poetic/genitive 'of' (or 'among'). شاهان گیتی = 'the kings of the world' (شاهان = kings, گیتی = world). برآورده is used adjectivally/participially meaning 'raised/upheld' or 'exalted' (archaic/poetic use), and سر 'head/chief' indicates preeminence. Together they mean 'exalted to the head among the world's kings.'
segments.s420-g37: نژادی functions as 'a lineage/stock' (poetic noun related to نژاد). ازین = از + این 'from/than this' (ازین = 'than this' in comparisons). نامورتر is the comparative 'more renowned'. کراست is an archaic copular/existential form (poetic), roughly 'is/there is'—it links the comparative predicate to the subject. The phrase asserts no lineage is more famous than this one.
segments.s420-g38: خردمند 'a wise man' is the subject. گردن 'neck' is used idiomatically for submission/bowing. نپیچد is the present negative form (ن + پیچد), here expressing a general truth/habitual 'does not turn' or 'will not turn'. ز راست = 'from the right/straight' meaning 'away from the straight/right way'. The clause says a wise person will not turn his neck away from what is right/straight.
segments.s420-g39: دگر 'other' introduces contrast. آنک is a relative pronoun 'who/which' referring forward to 'any other who'. اندر جهان سربسر = 'throughout the world' (اندر = in, جهان = world, سربسر = entirely/everywhere). The whole phrase sets up 'any other who everywhere in the world ...'.
segments.s420-g40: یلان 'heroes' is the object; را is the definite/direct-object marker. ز من 'from me' indicates source (who seeks heroes from me). جست here means 'seeks' (in older/poetic Persian جستن can mean to seek). باید 'it is necessary' (must/ought), and هنر 'skill, prowess' is the thing required. The clause states that anyone who seeks heroes of me needs skill/merit.
segments.s420-g25: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause ('that'). دریای چین is a genitive phrase 'the Sea of China' (دریا + proper name چین). تا میانش = 'as far as its middle' (تا = 'as far as/even', میانش = میان + -ش 'its middle'). بدی here is a poetic nominal predicate 'badness/harm' used predicatively to say there was harm up to the middle; the copula is omitted as is common in poetry.
segments.s420-g26: ز is the causal/instrumental 'from/because of'. تابیدن is the infinitive 'to shine' (here noun/gerund sense 'the shining'). خور is 'the sun' and زیانش 'its loss/detriment' (زیان + possessive -ش). بدی again is the noun 'badness/harm' — together the phrase means 'from the sun's shining its damage (was great)' (i.e. the sun's shining caused harm).
segments.s420-g27: همی is an emphatic/narrative adverb ('then/indeed' in epic register). ماهی is the direct object 'the fish'. از آب is the prepositional phrase 'out of the water' (از = 'from/out of', آب = 'water'). برداشتی is the past tense, 2nd person singular of برداشتن 'you lifted/took', so the clause reads 'you took the fish out of the water.'
segments.s420-g28: سر here is 'head'. از in the phrase سر از … is part of a locative/partitive expression ('the head on/from the …'); گنبد ماه is a genitive 'the dome of the moon' (گنبد = dome/vault, ماه = moon). بگذاشتی is the past 2nd person singular of گذاشتن 'you placed/put', so the clause means 'you set/placed the head upon the moon's dome.'
segments.s420-g29: به is an instrumental/agent marker 'by' here. خورشید = 'sun'. ماهیش is derived from ماه + -ش and functions adjectivally/possessively here ('its moon-ness' / 'that which belongs to the moon') — poetically linking the fish to the moon; بریان is an adjective/participle 'roasted/burnt', and شدی is past 2nd singular of شدن 'you became' (used here as 'you were roasted'). The clause thus says 'by the sun (relative to the moon) you were roasted.'
segments.s420-g30: ازو = 'from it' / 'because of that' (referring to the previous event). چرخ گردنده = 'the revolving sky/heaven' (چرخ = sky/heaven, گردنده = rotating/revolving). گریان is predicate adjective 'weeping/mourning' and شدی is the past of شدن 'became'. Together: 'Because of that, the revolving sky became weeping (mournful).' (Note: in poetic usage the person/ending can vary from prose norms; here شدی functions to mark the change of state 'became'.)
segments.s420-g31: دو is the numeral 'two'. پتیاره is an archaic/uncertain noun (the text preserves the word but its exact meaning is obscure; here it names the two beings). زین گونه is an idiomatic phrase 'in this manner/thus' (زین here functions as part of that fixed expression and not literally 'saddle'), and پیچان شدند is past plural 'they writhed/turned/twisted' (پیچان 'twisting', شدند = they became/were).
segments.s420-g32: ز is the causal/instrumental 'by/from'. تیغ یلی = 'the sword of the hero' (تیغ = sword, یلی used adjectivally/attributively 'valiant/heroic' or indicating the hero). هر دو = 'both' (literally 'each two' functioning as 'both of them'). بیجان شدند is 'they became lifeless/insensible' (بیجان = lifeless, شدند = they became). So: 'both became lifeless by the hero's sword.'
segments.s420-g17: This is a pair of locative noun phrases in classical word order: preposition + place (به دریا / به خشکی) followed by the animal placed there (نهنگ / پلنگ). The preposition به is used here with a locative/into sense ('in/on, at'); the two halves are joined by و, producing a poetic listing (‘in the sea a whale, and on the land a leopard’).
segments.s420-g18: ورا refers back to those creatures (an old pronominal form), and کس ندیدی is literally ‘you saw no one’ with the object (کس) before the past negative verb ندیدی — a common Persian order. گریان ز جنگ is a participial/ adjectival phrase ('fleeing from battle') postposed to modify کس. The whole clause reads: ‘of them/there you saw no one fleeing from battle.’
segments.s420-g19: به دریا is a locative phrase ('in the sea'). سر ماهیان is a genitival/part-whole expression 'the heads of the fishes' (سر = 'head' modified by ماهیان). برفروخت is a past tense verb used here in the sense 'was kindled/was set ablaze' or 'flared up' describing what happened to those heads; in poetry this verb can mean 'to blaze/shine' as well as 'to set on fire'.
segments.s420-g20: هم‌اندر is an adverbial connector meaning 'likewise/also', followed by the locative هوا ('in the air'). پر کرگس is a possessive grouping 'the vulture's feather(s)/plumage' (پر = 'feather'), with the verb بسوخت (past, 'burned/was burned') stating the result. Word order is typical poetic Persian: adverbial + place + possessed noun + verb.
segments.s420-g21: همی is a frequent poetic prefix indicating continuous or habitual action ('ever/still'); پیل را is the definite object marked by را. درکشیدی is past second-person singular of a compound verb here meaning 'drew/pulled' (در + کشیدن) rather than 'endure'. به دم is a temporal phrase 'for a moment' (دم = 'moment' in poetry). Together: 'you drew/pulled the elephant for a moment.'
segments.s420-g22: دل خرم is the subject phrase 'my/ the joyful heart' (دل = 'heart', خرم = 'joyful'). از یاد او is an ablative/cause phrase 'from/at the memory of him', شدم is the past first-person singular of شدن ('I became'), and دژم is the resulting predicate adjective 'sullen/grim'. Word order: subject – circumstance – verb – predicate, yielding 'my joyful heart became sullen because of remembering him.'
segments.s420-g23: و is the coordinating conjunction 'and' linking this clause to what came before. دیگری 'دیگر' here means 'another' and is followed by یکی, an indefinite 'one/a certain' that introduces the noun; together they mean 'another one' or 'a certain other'. دیو is the head noun 'demon', and the two following modifiers بد بدگمان form a compound epithet (adjectives follow the noun in Persian): بد 'bad/evil' plus بدگمان 'ill-disposed/suspicious' intensify the demon's malicious character. The whole phrase is a nominal phrase functioning as the subject/figure described by the next segment.
segments.s420-g24: This is a verbless locative description: تنش (تن + enclitic ش) = 'his body' with the possessive attached, followed by the preposition بر 'on/upon' and زمین 'the ground'. The conjunction و links the second coordinate phrase سرش (سر + ش = 'his head') and the prepositional phrase بآسمان (archaic spelling of به آسمان) 'to/toward the sky'. Persian often omits a copula in such spatial descriptions, so these two noun + preposition phrases together describe the demon's posture.
segments.s420-g9: همان is a demonstrative ('that/same') pointing back to the previously mentioned figure. The noun phrase سام پور نریمان is appositive: سام (proper name) followed by پور نریمان ('son of Nariman'). بدست here is a prepositional/agentive phrase 'by the hand of' and attaches to the noun phrase (i.e. ‘by Nariman’s hand’). Poetic word order places the attributive phrases directly after the name.
segments.s420-g10: نریمان is the agent/topic repeated. گرد is used as a poetic verb 'to go about, to roam' (here describing action: 'went about'). از کریمان بدست is another prepositional cluster: از 'from/of' + کریمان 'the generous (people)' and the final بدست again 'by the hand (of)'; the phrase therefore means roughly 'went about among the generous by that hand'. The clause is compact and elliptic, typical of Ferdowsi.
segments.s420-g11: بزرگست is an archaic/polite present copula meaning 'is great' (بزرگ + -ست). و simply coordinates. گرشاسپ بودش پدر is straightforward: گرشاسپ (proper name) + بودش, past-tense copula with enclitic -ش 'was his' (i.e. 'was his'), and پدر is appositive 'father' — 'Garshasp was his father.'
segments.s420-g12: به گیتی is a locative/phrase 'in the world' (به + گیتی). بدی here is a noun meaning 'evil' or 'ill' used predicatively; Ferdowsi often omits an explicit copula in such clauses, so the clause reads 'in the world (was) the evil…'. خسرو تاجور is a nominative apposition: خسرو (here a person/sovereign-name) qualified by تاجور — here an epithet meaning 'merchant/trader' (in this passage the epithet is used to characterize Khosrow).
segments.s420-g13: همانا is a vocative/adverb 'verily/truly'. شنیدستی is an archaic second‑person perfect/past ('you have heard' / 'hast thou heard?') addressing the listener/reader. آواز سام is the object phrase 'the voice/fame of Sam' (آواز here = 'fame/reputation/voice').
segments.s420-g14: نبد is an older/poetic contraction meaning 'was not / did not exist' (negated past existence). در زمانه = 'in the age' (در + زمانه). چنو is a demonstrative/adverb 'such/so' modifying the adjective نیک‌نام 'well‑reputed' — together: 'there was not in that age one so well‑reputed.'
segments.s420-g15: بکشتش is a past transitive verb with enclitic object -ش 'he killed him/it' (بکشت + -ش). The remaining phrase gives location: به طوس ('at/to Tus') and اندرون اژدها 'within the dragon' (اندرون = 'inside/within', اژدها = 'dragon/monster'), so the clause says he killed the dragon inside Tus (or 'he slew it, the dragon, within Tus').
segments.s420-g16: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'which/so that'. از چنگ او = 'from his claw/grip' (از = 'from', چنگ = 'claw/hand/hold', او = 'his'). کس نیابد رها is literal 'no one finds (نیابد) freed/released (رها)': نیابد here is negative/unstressed present/past sense 'finds/attains' used to mean 'cannot find', and رها functions as 'free/released'. Together: 'from his grip no one can get free.'
segments.s420-g65: The clause is inverted: 'دگر سام کو بود' is an interrogative/frame-setting phrase meaning 'where then/else was Sam' (کو after the noun marks 'where'). 'ما را نیا' places the patient/possessive 'ما' with the object marker 'را' before the noun 'نیا' (ancestor), preparing that noun to be the subject/agent of the following verb in the next segment.
segments.s420-g66: ببرد is the verbal core 'take/carry (away)'; in Ferdowsi's narrative style this present/subjunctive-shaped form functions as a past action 'carried away'. It takes the previously fronted 'ما را نیا' (our ancestor) as agent/patient relation: 'our ancestor carried me/us away from ...'. 'از جهان' marks source 'out of the world/of', and 'دانش و کیمیا' lists the sphere 'knowledge and alchemy'.
segments.s420-g67: سه دیگر here means 'three other/three more (instances)'. The relative/temporal clause 'که چون من ببستم کمر' = 'when/as I girded my loins' — 'چون' is used temporally ('when/as'), 'من' is subject 'I', and ببستم is the first-person past-form (the b- prefixed form common in epic diction) meaning 'I girded/tied' with 'کمر' (waist/loin) as its object.
segments.s420-g68: This is a result clause: 'تن آسان شد' literally 'the body became easy/at rest' — 'تن' can be collective (people) and 'آسان شد' is 'became secure/comfortable' (شد = became, past). 'اندر جهان تاجور' is a locative/nominal phrase 'in the world (اندر جهان) [for] the تاجور (merchants/traders)', so overall: when I girded my loins, the merchants' lives in the world became safe/peaceful.
segments.s420-g69: بران is a locative/adverbial 'on that / thereafter', followed by the compound 'خرمی روز' = 'a day of joy/delight'. 'هرگز نبود' is the negated past existential 'there was never' (هرگز = never, نبود = was not). Word order places the time/locative first, then the nominal 'day of joy', and finally the negated verb.
segments.s420-g70: This clause states that nobody thereafter followed a wrong path into theft. پی here means 'in pursuit of / following' (adverbial sense), 'مرد بی‌راه' = 'a man astray / a wrong-doer', 'بر' functions as 'toward' or 'into', 'دز' = 'thief' (i.e. thievery), and 'نبود' negates existence in the past ('there was not / no one was'). Together: 'no man pursued the way of wrongness toward thievery.'
segments.s420-g71: The subordinating 'که' introduces a temporal/causal clause: 'که من بودم اندر جهان کامران' = 'that/when I was (بودم) fortunate/prosperous (کامران) in the world (اندر جهان)'. Word order places the copula 'بودم' after the subject 'من', with کامران as predicative adjective.
segments.s420-g72: This is an existential/possessive construction: 'مرا بود' literally 'there was to me' = 'I had'. It is followed by the possessed items 'شمشیر و گرز گران' = 'a sword and a heavy mace' (گران postmodifies the weapons as 'heavy/weighty'). 'بود' is past 'there was', so the whole means 'I possessed a heavy sword and mace.'

sentences.s420-s21: I keep the same pledge to Kay Kavus from the first, namely that no one may bring an excuse against me.
sentences.s420-s22: The same covenant of Kay Khosrow the Just — that no one among the Kians girded his loins like him.
sentences.s420-s23: I have traveled all over the land, and I have slain many tyrant kings.
sentences.s420-s24: When I crossed the Jeyhun over the water, Afrasiab came from Turan into Chin.
sentences.s420-s25: I left Kay Kavus during the battle of Hamavaran and, alone, went to Mazandaran.
sentences.s420-s26: Neither Arzhang remained nor the White Demon; nor the scale, nor the offspring of Ghandi, nor Bid.
sentences.s420-s27: I continually pursued the king's son and slew the brave, the wise.
sentences.s420-s28: For there was never anyone like Sohrab who had ever been tested in strength, in manliness, and in battle.
sentences.s420-s29: Indeed it is more than five hundred years since I parted from Zāl's back.
sentences.s420-s30: I was always a champion in the world; there was one who knew both my outward and my hidden side.
sentences.s420-s31: Like Fereydun the well-born, who set a great crown upon his head.
sentences.s420-s32: He took Zahhāk down from the throne and consigned that head and his crown to the earth.
sentences.s420-s1: Rostam said to him, “Be calm — what are you saying, such unacceptable words?”
sentences.s420-s2: Your heart cherishes still more crookedness; your spirit continually grows proud because of the court.
sentences.s420-s3: You are such as befits kings; a king utters no words except what is true.
sentences.s420-s4: The sovereign knows that Sam’s deeds are great, and (that) he is learned and well‑reputed.
sentences.s420-s37: I said this so that you may know everything: you are king, and the rebels are like a flock.
sentences.s420-s38: You have entered a new age; even if only for a while, you share the glory of Keykhosrow.
sentences.s420-s39: You always look upon your own body in the world; you are not aware of hidden affairs.
sentences.s420-s40: When sayings became many, we drank wine; for wine and life we would break (scatter) thought.
sentences.s420-s17: My mother was that daughter of Mehrab; to her the land of India was prosperous.
sentences.s420-s18: Zahhak was his fifth forefather, exalted among the kings of the world.
sentences.s420-s19: There is no lineage more famed than this; a wise man will not turn his neck from the right (i.e. will not abandon the straight path).
sentences.s420-s20: As for any other who, throughout the whole world, seeks heroes from me, skill is required.
sentences.s420-s13: That the Sea of China, as far as its middle, suffered harm — the sun's shining did it damage.
sentences.s420-s14: Then you lifted the fish out of the water and placed its head upon the dome of the moon.
sentences.s420-s15: You were roasted by the sun's power of the moon; because of it the revolving sky became sorrowful.
sentences.s420-s16: Two pətīāre (an obscure/archaic pair of creatures) thus writhed and both, by the hero's sword, became lifeless.
sentences.s420-s9: At sea a whale and on land a leopard — you saw none of them fleeing from battle.
sentences.s420-s10: In the sea the heads of the fishes were kindled; likewise in the air the vulture's plumage burned.
sentences.s420-s11: You drew (pulled) the elephant for a moment; my joyful heart became sullen at the remembrance of him.
sentences.s420-s12: And another wicked, ill-disposed demon — his body on the ground and his head toward the sky.
sentences.s420-s5: That same Sam, son of Nariman — by Nariman's hand; Nariman went about among the generous by that hand.
sentences.s420-s6: He is great, and Garshasp was his father; in the world there was the evil (or ill) of Khosrow the merchant.
sentences.s420-s7: Truly, you have heard the fame of Sam; there was not in that age one so well‑reputed.
sentences.s420-s8: He slew it in Tus — the dragon within — for from his grip no one finds release.
sentences.s420-s33: Where then was Sam? Our ancestor had carried me away from the world of knowledge and alchemy.
sentences.s420-s34: On three other occasions, when I girded my loins, the merchants of the world lived in ease.
sentences.s420-s35: From then on there was never a day of joy; no man went astray into thievery.
sentences.s420-s36: For while I was prosperous in the world, I had a heavy sword and mace.
